---
title = "Templates"
---

# Templates everywhere

*Calepin* uses templates for nearly all text transformations and assembly operations. Every rendered element (a figure, a code block, a callout, a heading) passes through a template. The page wrapper that adds `<html>` or `\documentclass` is a template. The navbar, sidebar, and footer of a website are templates. If you can see it in the output, there is a template behind it.

This means customization is always the same operation: find the template, edit it. No need to learn Rust or Go or Lua. Just write plain text!

It also means that *Calepin* projects are super LLM-friendly. Just ask your agent what you need, and it will almost certainly get it right:

> Go to the `_calepin/partials` folder. Switch all the colors to a Studio Ghibli inspired pastel theme. Make the top navigation bar twice as wide. Align the footer text to the right.

{% include "source_tip" %}

# Jinja2

Templates use [Jinja2](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/) syntax (via [MiniJinja](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/minijinja), a Rust implementation). Jinja2 is one of the most popular templating systems in the world. It is easy to learn, powerful, and well-documented.

The core idea: write normal text, and mark locations where values should be inserted with double curly braces:

```
Hello, {{ name }}!
```

When the template is rendered with `name = "World"`, the output is `Hello, World!`. That is the basic mechanism. Everything else builds on it.

For a thorough introduction, see the [Jinja2 Template Designer Documentation](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/en/3.1.x/templates/).


# Partials

In *Calepin*, individual template files are called "partials." Each partial controls one piece of output. They live in `_calepin/partials/`, organized by format:

```
_calepin/partials/
  html/         # HTML partials (figure.html, callout.html, page.html, ...)
  latex/        # LaTeX partials (figure.tex, page.tex, ...)
  typst/        # Typst partials (figure.typ, page.typ, ...)
  website/      # Website layout partials (navbar.html, sidebar.html, ...)
  common/       # Format-agnostic partials (.jinja)
```

Override rules are simple: if `_calepin/partials/` exists, *Calepin* uses only those files. If it does not exist, *Calepin* uses only its built-in defaults. There is no merging or fallback chain. You either use the defaults, or you take full control.


# Element partials

Element partials control small, self-contained components. Here is the built-in emphasis template for HTML:

`html/emphasis.html`:

```html
<em>{{ text }}</em>
```

And for LaTeX:

`latex/emphasis.tex`:

```latex
\emph{ {{- text -}} }
```

That is the entire file. One line, one element. Every emphasis in the output passes through this template.


# Div partials

Fenced divs (`::: {.classname} ... :::`) use templates for larger structures like figures, callouts, theorems, and code blocks. These templates receive richer variables.

Here is the built-in figure template for Typst:

`typst/figure.typ`:

```
{%- set loc = cap_location | default("bottom") %}
#figure(
  image("{{src}}", width: {{width_attr | default("70%")}})
{%- if caption %}, caption: [{{caption}}]{%- endif %}
){% if label %} <{{label}}>{% endif %}
```

The template receives variables like `src`, `caption`, `label`, `width_attr`, and `cap_location`, which are set by *Calepin* based on the figure's attributes.


# Page assembly

The page template wraps the entire rendered body in a complete document. Here is a simplified version of the built-in LaTeX page template:

`latex/page.tex`:

```latex
\documentclass[11pt]{article}
\usepackage[T1]{fontenc}
\usepackage{graphicx,hyperref}
{{bib_preamble}}
{{preamble}}

{% if title %}\title{ {{-title-}} }{% endif %}
{% if author %}\author{ {{-author-}} }{% endif %}

\begin{document}
{% if title %}\maketitle{% endif %}

{{abstract}}
{{toc}}
{{body}}
{{appendix}}
{{bibliography}}
{{bib_end}}
\end{document}
```

Variables like `body`, `title`, `preamble`, `bibliography`, and `toc` are populated by *Calepin* before the template is rendered. Custom front matter fields are also available: a field `custom_footer = "Confidential"` in your document becomes `{{custom_footer}}` in the page template.


# More powerful features

Jinja2 goes far beyond simple variable insertion. Here are three features that are especially useful in templates.

Conditionals let you vary output by format or by the presence of a variable:

```
{% if caption %}
<figcaption>{{caption}}</figcaption>
{% endif %}
```

Loops iterate over lists:

```
{% for item in keywords %}
\keyword{ {{- item -}} }
{% endfor %}
```

Filters transform values inline. Jinja2 provides `upper`, `lower`, `trim`, `replace`, `default`, `length`, and many others:

```
{{ title | upper }}
{{ width | default("100%") }}
```

Again, see the [Jinja2 documentation](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/en/3.1.x/templates/) for the full syntax.
